title: No is allowed. No is whole.
subtitle: An affirmation for saying no without the apology attached
excerpt: For the moment when you want to say no to something and the no keeps arriving with an explanation, an apology, or a softer version attached. A plain statement for the no that doesn't need qualifying.

practice.whenToUse: Before or after a situation where a no is needed but arrives dressed in apology, softening, or over-explanation

content.text: The no that comes with an apology is already compromised. The no that arrives with three sentences of explanation has started to become a yes. An unqualified no is a complete sentence. Most people, and particularly most women, have been trained to produce the qualified version by default.
Say this three times, standing if you can:
No is allowed. No is whole.
Then practice the no you need to say. Say it once, out loud, with nothing attached after it. Notice the pull to add something. Notice that you don't have to.
